reviews.review_text: i dont really know how else to put other than its... fine. its not utter garbage, its not perfection. its just okay. get it on sale and its probably worth it if you enjoy bethesdas general game design choices. its nothing special and as usual Todd pumped the hype machine to hard. i enjoy it, and will probably play through it again some time soon as i haven't played since just after launch. one of those games where i wish i could give a thumbs sideways.

What were they thinking?
I am so conflicted with this game, on the one hand I think it's great and on the other it's such a disappointment.

"Explore the stars!" Yet someone else has already been there before you; even when you go to the edges of space, you will find other people and man-made objects. Bethesda does not understand why people enjoy No Man's Sky. Some planets you are the first to be on them, with alien life, that's a different design or colour palette from others. Yet in this game, everything is copied and pasted across EVERY planet.

An RNG/ procedural generation game at its core,
I'm a fan of RNG/PROCGEN in games; it helps with replayability and keeps the player engaged. But I have never seen it implemented so poorly in a video game until Starfield.

You have the tech to go truly crazy with the procgen, yet they do not. If you've seen one planet, you have seen them all; it's just rocks with prefab POIs placed in it. And if you're lucky, one of the four alien wildlife.

The POIs are the same, literally the same, copied and pasted around the universe. Same layout, same placement for the enemies, same locked chests in the same places.

It's so wild to me that they think this is OK, the POIs are very blocky in design, so it would be easy to have RNG map layouts, but they do not, at least make a few templates and alternate between them, not just the one.

As others have said, this is a "loading screen simulator". And I would agree, fast travel here, then fast travel there. While they did add being able to fly your ship from one star system to another, it's pointless.

Story and quests,
I will not say much about the main story; while I did enjoy it, it's not the best, and it's handled strangely. I can't say anything else without spoiling.
Side content is interesting, with well-thought-out characters and SOME compelling storylines. But most boil down to "to go here, get or kill this, come back". In a future where cross-galaxy communication exists, why do I have to travel back to speak with the quest giver?

Companions and player choice,
Of the companions, you can get no one who really stands out from the rest; they all somewhat act the same. Redditors and "le wholesome group therapy sessions in human form".
All you ever hear is "I'm here for you" "I've never had a friend like you before."

It would have been nice if one or two were like that, but not all of them, no one has any edge or different personality, it's all very "safe HR speech".

Gameplay,
A faster-paced Fallout 4 with jumpacks, there you go, I've summed it up, it's not a bad thing, but that's the easiest way to explain.

While I do like the design of the enemies, there is such a lack of them that it's unreal. They made a whole galaxy and filled it with 4 types of human enemies, 4 or 5 types of alien wildlife, and 2 types of robots. I'm not joking, it really is that low. They could have made whatever their minds came up with, and that's all?
WOW

The Steam rating is right, mixed is right for this game.

Get on a deep sale,

For me, it's around a 6/10,
Somewhat above average, and you most likely will have fun for one run of the game, and most likely you will never play it again.

I did finish and mostly enjoyed my time with it. I will most likely never play it again, which is a shame, as I have replayed Fallout and Elder Scrolls many times. It has moments of greatness, but I am disappointed in this game. Maybe they can improve in a second game, but I'm not holding my breath.

I have it only because I forgot to return it. I forgot to return it because the first 3-4 hours of the game are pretty engaging. All the other hours are just so boring and repetitive. Whichever corner of the world you'll visit you meet exactly same dressed enemies, with the same weapons, in the same buildings as at the very opposite end of the map. The whole gameplay is about doing the same thing over and over and over. Very limited weapon choice for a game set in space. There is like 7 unique locations and the rest is rinse and repeat, same thing over and over. If someone would force me to pick up that damned floating glitter in that damned towers I would just go mad and never recover. It will be worth the price at 25% of the current one.

I held off on playing this game for a long time due to the reviews. Now that I am playing it, it is one of my favorite games of all times. The quality of content and game is uneven though. It has some truly amazing aspects, and some not so great ones as well. It is not perfect, but what it does do well is so worth it. Like all Bethesda games it needs mods to fix it and make it great.

Exploration and open world while making in challenging if need be or build your guy or gal on Super human abilities. The storyline adds to replayability and the Bethesda creations and ship/outpost building is fantatstic as all bethesda games are. This is a fantatsic game/story that can be played a million different ways.

I've been playing Bethesda games since TES:Arena. I've tolerated so much for so long, all in service to what seemed like a progression to repeatable and enjoyable generated game-play. Starfield, though, takes several steps back. The list of problems with this game is extensive, so I'll keep it brief and applicable to the latest DLC (Terran Armada):

1) Failing a persuasion check traps you in a side quest, forcing you to reload. 
2) NPC standing in front of you in cockpit traps you in your ship.
3) Enemy AI is beyond stupid. It either shoots at you or it runs to the ends of the map. 
4) "Stuck" points exist all over the game on terrain, but are most prominent indoors, effectively trapping you if some enemy locked you "in combat" and then ran halfway around the planet.

I just can't do it anymore. These games are getting worse and worse, and no matter how much I try, I can't stop the tsunami of disappointment that I feel whenever I try to do anything in this game only to run into reload bugs that an intern could fix if Bethesda(MSFT) even cared enough, which they clearly [b]do not[/b]. 

They received so much community attention in Skyrim that they assumed they didn't need to fix anything. They were wrong, and they've finally broken me of ever, ever buying a game they make again. I loved the Elder Scrolls games but after seeing how badly they did Starfield, I can only imagine the inescapable trainwreck that TES 6 is going to be.

Todd Howard and all of his developers are no better than used car salesman. Caveat emptor.

It is a lifeless, boring, forgettable game.

It has all of the features of a Bethesda game but lacks an interesting world and story. 
Has crusty star trek vibes. Like this game has the aesthetic appeal of Jensen Huang's leather jacket. 

You will probably be sick of this within the first few hours and if you played the other titles you are just going to dread it even more cause you are probably addicted to opening the boxes and finding the LOOT (like me). 

Don't buy this, It is a husk of a game - just play Fallout, Skyrim, etc.  if you are trying to scratch that itch.

I personally really enjoyed this game. Some things can feel a bit repetitive, but overall, I thought it was a blast exploring the various worlds and cities. There were some pretty neat side quests as well. I also enjoyed the combat. I never played with mods, but there are a decent number of them out there that you could check out as well. If you like Bethesda games, you should check it out. If you don't like sci-fi style games, it's probably not for you.
This game is like if ChatGPT made Space Skyrim with less content.

got on after not playing for a year to see how the free lanes update was and wow what a bummer game hasn't seemed to improved any and all the interesting added content is locked behind creations paywall
tbh the hype behind this game was so big. but such a huge  bummer that the game is dookie buns
It's terrible. Bethesda has learned absolutely nothing in the past 15 years. As it is this will be the last Bethesda game I will ever waste my money on.

I’ve given this game three chances already, but I still haven’t been able to finish the main story. I’d rather play through Skyrim or Fallout a few more times than start playing this again. From the terrible interface and crappy inventory system to the boring quests and dialogue, they have managed to make almost everything unplayable.

[h1]The gaming community has forgotten how to enjoy games.[/h1] 

Since when did we start relying more on the opinions of influencers than on our own? Starfield is probably the most hated game in history, but is it deserved? To write a competent review, you must first identify its genre. We can't just say a game is terrible because “it's not what I personally wanted.”

Twenty years ago, a game called TES Oblivion was released. But that’s not entirely true, it was actually Skyrim. And a couple of years later, Skyrim with guns came out, and then the real Skyrim came out, and then Skyrim with guns came out again. And for all those 20 years, we played these games and were happy. 

Todd Howard created his own genre — the "Bethesda RPG." What does that mean? His games are fundamentally Diablo-like sandboxes where the main activity is clearing handcrafted dungeons. The RPG elements simply give us different ways to level up and clear those areas. 

BGS games have never focused on Hollywood plots, deep dialogue, or cinematic exploration. They are built on a simple principle: “Go bring me my family shield from the cave, and in return, I’ll give you my best sword, which you’ll then sell to a merchant for 100 coins.” 

What a surprise when, after an hour-long presentation detailing everything, sneaky Todd Howard did it again and released Skyrim in space. Not a space simulator, not a dialogue-driven RPG, not a Souls-like, not GTA, not a story-driven action game—but good old Skyrim.

[h1]Gameplay[/h1] 

Starfield is a typical Bethesda game about clearing dungeons in space. The shooting is decent, with a wide variety of weapons, armor, and mods. The progression system feels better than in F4 or Skyrim, though it needs some polish. The dungeons themselves are great—huge, detailed, and packed with secrets, though I’d love to see more complex ones like Forgotten Vale. 

As a sandbox, it’s packed with activities: quests, ship-building, outpost decorating, bounty hunting, cargo delivery, mining, resource gathering, farming, exploration, piracy, and much more. But you DON’T HAVE TO do any of it. Todd's games don't hold your hand; if you don't like a mechanic, just move on to something else.

[h1]Space and Exploration[/h1] 

Starfield is NOT a space simulator. No one promised you that. The only major difference from previous BGS titles is the lack of a single world map, which is physically impossible in space.

 In Skyrim, you walk to a cave and get attacked by a bear. In Starfield, you fast-travel to a planet, enter a handcrafted cave on a procedurally generated world, and get attacked by pirates in orbit. How is this fundamentally different?

All core content—cities, POIs, and quests is handcrafted. Random generation just decides where to place them. The cities are massive, full of local stories and encounters. 

People complain about space travel, but space is EMPTY. You don’t cruise manually; you jump via wormholes. Flying through a loading screen makes physical sense because actual space travel would take months or years. Even sending a radio message across star systems would take years, which is why NPCs ask you to deliver letters in person.

Furthermore, the planet tech is impressive. Planets orbit stars and moons orbit planets in real time with dynamic lighting, biomes, and gravity. (Unlike No Man's Sky, where the skybox merely rotates around fixed planets). In the end, you get orbital fights and gravity jumps. What more could you ask for from a game about clearing dungeons?

[h1]Quests[/h1] 

Storytelling has never been Bethesda's main strength, but Starfield is a huge leap forward. The main quest and major faction lines are well done. There are radiant quests, but they serve to help you level up and explore. Quests often feature multiple endings, role-playing dialogue, and a solid persuasion system. I especially enjoyed the local stories in the Shattered Space and Free Lanes updates.

[h1]Engine, Graphics, and Optimization[/h1] 

Why the hate for the Creation Engine? It runs thousands of mods with RELATIVE stability and offers a complete platform for modders. It allows hundreds of NPCs to have independent schedules, inventories, and AI routines. Locations contain thousands of physical objects that respond to lighting and physics. You can build massive ships and decorate them inside. You can actually "touch the worlds" in Todd Howard's games. No other engine offers this level of interactivity.

The cost of this freedom is a 1-second loading screen.

It is physically impossible to keep this many interactive objects in memory without them; any PC would crash. Besides, Starfield's cities are entirely open, and half the interiors don't require loading screens anyway.

Starfield is a 2023 game, and as Todd said (I’m serious), “just upgrade your PC.” A game of this scale requires at least a 6-core/12-thread CPU and a 2016 Card with 8GB of VRAM. Is that really so much to ask? The game has one of the most advanced lighting systems I’ve ever seen, alongside insanely high-quality textures and models. Personally, on a mid-range graphics card from 2023 (RTX 4060), I have 100–200 FPS. Throughout my entire playthrough, I never encountered a broken quest glitch, and the game only crashed a couple of times due to heavy modding.

Motion capture isn't used because there are hundreds of sandbox NPCs, but their expressions and gestures look decent and remain highly modder-friendly.

[h1]NASA-punk[/h1]

The main reason some people might not like Starfield is the setting, and I get that. Some people were expecting a space fantasy like Star Wars, Mass Effect, or Star Trek. But no. Starfield is closer to Interstellar, Aliens, Starship Troopers, Event Horizon, or 2001: A Space Odyssey. There are no intelligent humanoid aliens here. And I like that—it makes the universe feel more serious, scientific, and mature.

The game doesn't feature massive, galaxy-spanning space wars or cinematic spectacles. It's a typical Bethesda game, all about cozy, local stories—but this time, set in space.

[h1]CONS[/h1]

Starfield isn't perfect—no game is. Some quests could have been better and more in-depth; occasionally, you come across ones that feel lazy and half-made. The progression system could have been better, and I'd like to see more large, unique POIs. The game also had a rocky launch, needing more initial polish and quality-of-life improvements.

But the biggest issue for me is modding.

The problem is that Starfield really wants to be a “field” for mods. While there are already a lot of amazing mods out there, a massive technical issue has been discovered in the game: the “pointer handle limit.” 

The thing is, this limit hasn’t changed since Fallout 4. The vanilla version of Starfield, even without any mods installed, already takes up more than half of that limit. Therefore, running large mod packs over a long playthrough can completely break your save file. The game will start crashing and corrupting quests or locations.

What's worse, Bethesda seems to be ignoring this (at least, I haven't seen an official response) while simultaneously selling official Creations/mods without warning players or mod authors that the game might eventually break.

This is a massive issue that threatens Starfield’s long-term future. If it isn’t resolved, the game will soon no longer be able to handle either new community mods or official DLCs.

[h1]Conclusion[/h1]

Starfield is far from perfect, but it absolutely doesn’t deserve the overwhelming hate it receives. Right now, it’s Bethesda’s most impressive game with a great future ahead of it—but only if they fix the mod limit issue. For now, I’d give it a solid 7.5/10.

I want to like this game, but the user interface is terrible.  Keyboard operators are not overloaded, so there's a different command for EVERYthing.  I mouse left-handed and keyboard right-handed, so I tried rebinding the controls.  There's always some other intermediate interface with its own keys, so it's all a confusing hodgepodge.  I came back to it after 2.5 years, hoping for improvement, but there is none.  I'm writing this review after reloading a save point from before I chose an outpost landing site.  The interface will NOT display the surface resources, despite toggling that scan a few times.  I'm uninstalling now.  Good luck, Bethesda, and ES6 needs to be a LOT better game engine than this.  Take a look at No Man's Sky if you want a good example of a simplified interface that does everything (ship combat, star hopping, and base creation) with a significantly streamlined graphic user interface.
Still critical bugs in end game missions that were there from launch.  What a joke
The game doesn’t deserve the amount of hate that it gets. It’s not a masterpiece, and it did have the potential to be much better, but it’s still solid, especially if you catch it on sale.
The story was great , and the new game plus feature tieing into the story sum is a nice touch. if you into sci fi , this is a huge recomendation.

Boring exploration, dead copy-pasted planets. 
Stupid dialogues, characters are hollow and have no moral depth, choices barely matter.
Greedy monetization via Creation Club.
Outdated game.

I am one of those people who has played hundreds and hundreds of hours of Skyrim, with various mods on four different platforms, but I’ve never finished the main quest.

Starfield feels like Skyrim, but in space.

Much like Skyrim:
* The opening sequence is pretty linear, though you could go off-script fairly quickly if you want. If you restart the game a bunch, it might get repetitive.
* after the opening sequence, you can choose to focus on the main story line, on NPC arcs, or you can do whatever the hell you want. It doesn’t really hold your hands. Someone might feel overwhelmed by the options or aimless, but I think it’s cool.
* there are SO MANY useless items that you can weigh yourself down with. (Spoon collectors?)
* crafting is a big thing if you want it to be, but you could reasonably play the game without crafting much at all. You probably wouldn’t be as interested in the survey quests though.
* NPCs are pretty stupid sometimes. You can hide from pirates in their own compound after they already know you’re there.
* there are “bad guys” everywhere without any moral question that it’s okay to kill them. I am a mass murderer without a bounty

Unlike Skyrim:
* the player universe is ridiculously massive. You could get lost in this game for years. 
* Everywhere you go there are random side quests given to you by nameless characters, (because the game is too massive to give everyone a story unlike in Skyrim) and those can get repetitive, but it gives you something to do while you “survey” solar systems.
* the crafting system is more complicated and can go industrial
* There’s scientific underpinnings to the crafting system that might surprise or delight you (or maybe disappoint you if you’re more of a chemist than I am). Carboxysilanes as a harvestable resource. Seriously. Planets have collections of resources that radioactively decay to each other (like uranium, plutonium, vanadium, etc)
* the skill tree is different than any RPG I’ve seen. You need to level up crafting skills to be able to craft things.
* the tutorials are minimal to nonexistent. I feel like I’m “figuring it out without reading a user manual” sometimes
* every follower character has a back story, extensive side quests, and motivations. No more stone-faced “I am sworn to carry your burdens” Lydia.

Overall, if you love crafting and exploring, and if you enjoy sci-fi worlds, you’ll probably like this game.

If you are expecting a game that will capture your full attention, build suspense and plot for you, or tell a coherent singular story, then you might be disappointed.

Game is a lot better now. Free Lanes and POI adjustments bring back a decent amount of the "journey beats the destination" that is so important in Bethesda games. Add in some mods and general improvement and I am enjoying this much more than when it first launched. 

 There is still wasted potential. I find the small size and general lack of settlements to be a big disappointment, the world building leaves some to be desired. The NPCs appearance is uncanny and a big step back from Fallout 4/Skyrim, and a lot of the side quests are pretty ho-hum.

But overall, this now delivers on "Bethesda RPG in space" pretty well, and I'm having a blast on my second playthrough.
This game gets a lot of hate. I enjoy the solitude, and just walking around on all the beautiful planets. It revitalized that childhood love i had for space.
This game felt clunky and unalive. Maybe the missions improve as the game goes on, but I just lost interest.

I bought it again recently because I saw a Star Wars mod for it. It was nice, but even with that mod I seriously could not get into the game again. I despise the UI and the game doesn't feel right, I cannot place it. It's just not fun.

Deep story, good gameplay... I really don't get why this game gets the hate it did other than, it wasn't a new Fallout... 
I REALLY like this game.
As a FO4 fan and Star Wars fan, Starfield is my new love. Open world, ship and base building, and mods to turn it into the best Star Wars game that never was.

Empty planets and reuse of locations.
Lacklustre shooting mechanics and boring enemies.

Bethesda made great promises that they failed to achieve, but rather than take critique they say the customer cannot appriciate the "uninhabited planet filled with nothing".
I would say more but this is the extent of how much i care, i got more important things to do.

I'm having a problem trying to access the "Ship jacker " Mission (one of the Trackers alliance Mission) on the Main bounty board. previously I was able to accept it with Ease, but After the update of the content I Can't access the mission, because the bounty board can't cycle left or right no matter what button I push... Now I like some of Bethesda's games, but they really need to fix their games and make sure they are full proofing their game's of mission errors because....
I CAN'T PLAY "THE TRACKERS ALLIANCE" CONTENT in general

(Do not go off my Steam time in game, I have around 80 hours on Xbox Cloud)

This is a difficult review to write. Mostly because I wrote off ever playing this game until it showed up as playable on Xbox cloud and since I already pay for the service, it made sense to give it a try. I remember when the game launched and it was poorly received and it seems as if the sentiment is still present. Some of the reviews I have seen have legitimate concerns about game features and others think the game is just not fun. Both fair points but I have my own opinions on it. So take it with a grain of salt. 

For my review, I will focus on what I experienced in the game and my thoughts. 

The game starts off like any Bethesda game. You go through a few basic motions, get your feet wet with the main quest, and then you go around doing literally anything other than the main quest. For reference, I went to New Atlantis and dropped off the piece and that was it. I have done as much as I possibly can other than the main quest. 

Combat: Harsh. A bit simple. But follows the formula for Bethesda games. You have a weak weapon, enemies are bullet sponges. You get a cool weapon later and level up a bit and get some perks, now you're the ultimate badass. That's how it's always been and how it's stayed. I have no complaints about that and honestly, it's what I expected. I will try not to reference any negative reviews I've read but I feel I will have to just to explain that I never once felt combat was awful. It wasn't the best combat mechanics I've seen in a game but it never pulled me out. I think it is still largely enjoyable and well within what I was expecting. 

Space Travel: This I have spent a long time doing. I land on planets. Explore. Scan. Maybe build an outpost. Maybe not. But I've never not enjoyed it. For reference, I play Elite Dangerous and Eve: Online. I have a love/hate relationship with both. Though I am partial to Elite Dangerous even if it feels like having a second job at times (If you play it you'll know what I'm talking about). I can explore entire systems in a single gaming session in Starfield as opposed to a single planet every hour in Elite Dangerous. Which brings me to my next point. One of those games is a simulator and the other is an RPG. Starfield is not a simulator. It's an RPG and it's supposed to be fun and accessible, which it is most certainly both. It never felt tedious and I also never expected to have fully populated planets teaming with life and cities. I expected what I got. On a side note: I still do not understand the point of Caves. I avoid them every single time unless I really need to find water and it's usually there. 

Lore: This is the first original universe Bethesda has created in a long time. It feels like it. The lore is there but its lived in and not screamed at you. That's fine. I actually found the game setting and lore to be fine with me. I didn't find it lacking. It does borrow from a multitude of Sci-fi tropes which... That's to be expected. As I haven't really started the main quest I don't know what that looks like. But the factions are fine. I don't really care who they are and what they stand for. But I didn't the first time I played Morrowind either. I didn't get into the game lore really until Oblivion and then Skyrim got us all in the Imperial or Stormcloak camps. I'm fine with it. I really want a sequel as I think the universe will deepen a bit more which is what I'm hoping for. But for an entry into a new IP, I think it did its job. 

Gripes and complaints: This didn't feel like Skyrim in space. Which is fine. But it felt like Fallout 4 in space. This isn't a complaint really. But I did not like Fallout 4. I never finished it and have no intention of finishing it. As many times as I've tried to get into, I cannot. I do not think Fallout 4 is a bad game. But its not fun for me. Starfield is a blast for me. Which is where my major point is... My idea of a perfect game... What I would love to see... Is where I can go anywhere, do anything, and my actions have real world impacts. If I go to a location and set fire to their fields. They should starve, move, or the price of crops should shoot up. If I kill an entire royal family, there should be a succession crisis immediately and I could join the fight and pick who I want to rule. Or rule myself. That would be a true sandbox experience for me and to me, that would be perfection in a game. Where I can do anything I want and change the game world however I feel that day. I do not think that will happen in my lifetime. Though if it does that will the only game I'd ever play. That being said... I do not know what people expected from this game. But I thought it was fun. It's not perfect. It's Bethesda. We expect a quirky sandbox game with some crazy stuff to do and able to do every faction quest available. Which we can. The choices you make in this game are part of your story. They won't effect the game and that's the point, it's about how you want to play while keeping the world open to explore and not shutting you out. That's what I get from it.
It's just not a very fun game. I was hoping it might take better shape after the release but it looks like the updates never really amounted to much. Pretty disappointed.

Please don't ask me how I managed 325 hours on this game. For some daft reason I've done it from start to finish twice.

You'd think then, that I'd recommend it then? No. I don't.

I've more than had my money's worth - on that there is no debate - but was I satisfied with the experience? No, I wasn't. It's a very mediocre game as a lot of the comments point out.

For years I've been a Bethesda fan in the days where what they did was envelope pushing even with all the cracks and glitches but this, after the hype is all the same problems with a graphical improvement - that many games surpassed at light speed I might add.

I played this before I finally played No Man Sky - in fact, it was my interest in this that had people telling me to play No Man Sky, and I liked it better. Hell, I might even say that Star Citizen is better than this game and that's an eternal alpha. Looks good though and isn't a loading screen simulator.

Of all the most damning things I could say about this game, and there are many (ranging from gameplay, characters, storyline, world building and on and on) the thing I would say is that this triggered a feeling in me that I can't shake.

I think Bethesda's time is up. Too much hubris from Todd. Too wed to their platform to understand that they must evolve or die. Too dependent on glitch forgiveness - when other studies are moving at pace to bring their games up to incredible levels.

I don't imagine that Bethesda have it in them to move the needle forward anymore. Sure, they'll drop a new Fallout or Elder Scrolls some day - but you can bet it will be on this engine, with limited graphics.

I hope I'm wrong and they do something amazing but I'm doubtful.

Anyway, thanks for the time I got from this game - like I said, I got my moneys worth - whatever that means - but I don't recommend it.
very fun for space exploation. the graphics are 10/10 the story is like a 6/10 but the side quests and game mechnicas are 11/10

8/10; It's definitely not Bethesda's best work by any means, but form your own opinion about it, don't listen to the crowd. I bought this game day one and could never get into it until recently, and it ended up being pretty good. Does it have the same depth as Fallout or The Elder Scrolls? No, but it's doing its own thing and if you enjoy space, you may find this game enjoyable. However, I would not buy at full price.

As I have just gotten the bug where I can no longer target ships, playthrough is now trashed as I cannot board the ships I need to to finish the Terran Armada DLC, or most space based parts of the game.  Looking for a work around, which none of them worked, also shows that this is at least a 2023 issue.  Shockingly (sarcasm) no hot fix from Bethesda in 3 years, to fix a pretty game breaking issue.

A decent game, not sure why there are so many people hating it.

I spose if you compare each and every aspect of the game to another you'll always find a paticular game that is better for the givin aspect but same goes with all games.

[h1]Is the game all that was hyped? no: [/h1]
- Dialog is cheesy and often pointless
- Most decisions have little consequence or silly consequences
- Immersion is sometimes broken pointessly (I see you grav-drive-jump-loading & locations with no outside views)
- Bethesda greediness on pricing and mod management (mod achievements friendliness is behind a paywall)
- Buggy there and there
BUT
[h1]Is the game bad? also no, quit the opposite:[/h1]
- Full of amazing details (graphical or otherwise)
- Full of lore and colorful characters
- Fun shooting
- Fun armors and perks
- Fun ship building and ship flying
- The modding community brings super impressive stuff (I dont mind pay when the creator did a good job and free versions of almost everything are out there if you dont care or are done about achievements
- Fallout 4 in space with huge quality of life improvements over it

Its not about what you cannot do. Its about expectations: this is not a space sim, nor should have never been announced or hinted like that, its a openish-world-light-rpg full of easy fun at bethesda-fallout style. The game delivers so much to do and to try that is difficult to be bored. Then if you are bored the mods come along and suddently you have a new arsenal, new ship parts, new armors and new stories to try. All mixable so you can experience all the vanilla and modded ingredients in different new ways.

Please avoid. The quests are notoriously buggy and they refuse to update the game causing mass frusteration. This game had potential but they botched it. Bethesda is washed. Coming from a true bethesda fan of the past who played everygame since daggerfall. Washed. avoid like the plague. for real.

I have loved this game since it came out. It’s definitely my comfort game, just to get on, mess around with mods and ship building and quests. Definitely recommend for those who want a Sci-Fi experience where you can become whatever you want.
As is tradition with Bethesda games, game breaking bug that prevents you from playing the game. The game crashes when opening doors... wtf.

It's definitely a divisive game. Personally the only thing worse than a bad game is a boring game. This is utterly boring and lackluster in comparison to other Bethesda games. 
It's a 5/10. Just mediocre/average, and don't even get me started on how much Shattered Space let me down, plenty just feels like you had no choice in the end.

If I had to say something positive I like idea of having to complete a challenge before upgrading a skill and I didn't encounter many bugs until after finished the main quest which just had everyone missing torsos including robots.

I think everyone can agree that starfield was a soup sandwich on launch....So was cyber punk. However, after much polishing and refinement, the game is now impressive...just like cyberpunk... I think starfield is honestly beyond words. It's jaw dropping. No cookie cutter layouts. YOu can feel the authenticity of the devs when creating this game. It is unbelievable. There are nearly 2,000 different plants/moons to explore. The vehicles is the catalyst to enjoying this game. I did not like starfield before because there were these massive planets with nothing to do. Now, at least with the vehicles you can zip around no problem and can enjoy the game without falling asleep while walking forward for the last 45 minutes. The game is legendary in my opinion. Give it another chance. Take it from me

Goated Game that I feel gets hated.
it has its faults and is worst than Skyrim and Fallout but its still a great game that at least should be given a chance if you like the Bethesda games.
Imo its like 8/10 type game

If you are the "find your own fun" type of person who can come up with things to do in a sandbox that doesn't really care about your involvement in it all that much, you may find some value in this game. I'm not that kinda person.

I'll give it to the devs, Starfield makes hours disappear... by making you do uninteresting work. You enter a procedurally dropped POI that you've seen a few dozen times and then get into this loop of killing/looting followed by selling/crafting, on repeat, forever. This isn't inherently bad IF you do something exciting with the stuff you crafted and looted. You don't, cause the writers decided to use Emil's famous "Keep It Simple Stupid" method here in full force. Most of the time, I'm either emotion coaching grown adults, or doing some quest that changes nothing in the world. You can literally walk away from 90% of the quests in this game and the world wouldn't even notice your departure. You can do or choose whatever, and things will broadly work out and nothing major will break. It's a feel-good game... no, more like feel-kinda-ok game.

Bottom line, Starfield is average to play if you have nothing else to do while you wait for real heavy hitters to come out. It's "ok" for that kinda thing. Just make sure to find a deal/sale cause what's here is not a game worth the full price. You can get way more value investing that cash in something else.

Anytime I see Starfield in my library, I check back to see the reviews and am always entertained. I find it hilarious that I somehow played this game for 99 hours and my mental psyche was like "Yeah sorry I dont think I can play another hour of this game again." Bethesda is looking down the barrel right now and that trigger is getting pulled if Elder Scrolls is a flop and the next Fallout is more of the same we can expect..

The biggest problem with Bethesda is their inability to adapt and change. Over time the industry moves forward, things get better, but for Bethesda, no. They are stuck in the early 2010s with their design, from Quests to story and their reluctance to move past their outdated game engine!
 where to begin?
-NOTHING MATTERS! in a supposedly RPG game where i should be able to make decisions, want to kill NPCs? well nope you cant do that. want to make a evil choice well sorry the quest will reload to previous auto save so you can choose the correct choice besthesdas design team wants you to choose. they shoe horn you into  certain paths. i cant be the bad guy. its like the design is a checklist. you completed this part of the quest make this decision and everything resets back to factory settings. now do next quest. there is no flow no consequence to actions. nps dont remember anything...
-You can do anything you want in 1 playthrough. if i choose to be a pirate, i can also be a free star ranger and save the galaxy.. what? quests should be blocked for a playthrough based on certain decisions i make for current run. this is one problem with bethesdas design philosophy, they dont want to say no to you 
- Bland often robotic NPCs. everyone sounds monotone with no personality. honestly at some parts i thought they were all AI voiced. really bad and i was often falling asleep at certain parts 
-Story Absolutely ATROSIOUS! seriously if this is the pinnacle design they can come up with i have serious doubts about their upcoming games. they are hamstrung, devout of any inspiration. From the base game to the DLCs
-DLCs boring with no originality. often repeat boring fetch quests...
i have serious doubts with their upcoming games. they need to adjust their thinking and move past the 2010 or else they are doomed to fail.

I enjoyed my time playing Starfield. I like the Bethesda style of being able to mess around in the world, joining factions and participating in world altering storylines

In Starfield, exploration is not as cohesive as in Elder Scrolls and Fallout it must be said. There may be 1000 odd planets but each planet does not have that much to explore and exploration can be more menu driven compared to Bethesda's other games where you could go travel the world in any direction you choose.

That aside, I enjoyed the various sidequests and faction plots, with Sysdef/Crimson Fleet being the standout faction-wise featuring double agency, piracy, potential treasure and a fun time. A lot of the sidequests feel interesting to play on a first-time playthrough; stuff like meeting a rogue AI, figuring out what to do with a bunch of early explorers out of earth, uncovering the mystery of a vigilante hero are some ones off the top of my head which I liked. I might make it sound cooler than these quests were in the game, but I felt there were different and interesting sub stories in the game.

The game also has mission board style quests where you have repeatable quests for bounty hunting, delivering goods and maybe for other jobs that I don't remember. They mostly play in the same standard way, but if you feel comfortable having that game loop where you can roleplay as a bounty hunter or a trader, you can do it to some extent.

I liked the main quest, I didn't really like the idea of the story being hunting artifacts at first, but as the story progressed to the Starborn and the stakes seemed to get more serious for Constellation, I warmed up to it. I liked Constellation, from the 4 companions to the homebodies, they were a cool main cast and I wish the game featured Matteo and Noel a bit more tbh. The story does get piled on usually and the Starborn temple puzzles could have been more interesting, but IMO its a nice enough storyline that does its job of feeling high-stakes and grandiose.

I dig the NASAPunk vibe Starfield went with; this more grounded sci-fi style and an optimistic like tone to the world, compared to the space opera style of other space games/shows and the more cynical like tone of the Fallout series.

The DLCs were okay. Bethesda said of Terran Armada that if you like Starfield already, it'll be more of the same and if you didn't, it wont change your mind either. I feel that applies to Shattered Space too.

My playtime was almost entirely on a non-steam version of the game, I thought I'd get the steam version for Terran Armada but 5000 rupees was too big a dent in my wallet sadly. I do appreciate the heck outta this game and am glad I can leave a review about my thoughts on here regardless.

Bought this when it came out, got premium pack and everything. Was very disappointed, gunplay was not horrible in relation to other bethesda titles. The fast travel system was broken many other  mechanics just seemed like a chore to learn, but yet i tried it. I have 32 hours on record and that was from 3 years ago with one playthrough. I downloaded it today to try it with the new updates not realizing that all the updates are packed into DLCs that i now need to purchase to enjoy... so back into the bin with you Starfield.
this game constantly crashes... sometime it takes my computer out with it... what in the actual ♥♥♥♥..

This game has its ups and downs. A lot, if not all of the criticism is valid, but there are things to like about the game. I think my biggest issue with Starfield is the pacing. Hard to pick it back up after taking a break.

Though everything I would say has already been said a million times, so I won't regurgitate, just know it's mostly true.

I completely wasted my money on Starfield. We were promised years of top-tier support, but that turned out to be a lie. The game has been a massive disappointment since launch, and Bethesda seemingly has no intention of putting forth the effort to turn it around. Look at Cyberpunk 2077: CD Projekt Red launched a broken game but stuck to their guns and completely fixed it until it became great. Bethesda has the resources and the ability to put their best foot forward. Starfield has the bones to be amazing, and the original intentions were right, so I don't know why they dropped the ball so completely.

Pros: combat is okay, space ship fights are great, huge open world, plenty to explore

Cons: repetitive, nonexistent stealth, horrible economy, flat characters and story

Overall: This game really felt like a master of none. Everything is serviceable but nothing is memorable. The space fights feel really good but they weren't good enough to overcome all the other issues. The combat is okay but it gets stale fairly quick. I was able to upgrade my weapons by level 20 to carry me for the rest of the game. At that point combat basically just became point and click with no real challenge. This also made collecting loot unexciting since everything I was picking up was worse. The economy is horrible, the loot you pick up has a high price and all the merchants can maybe buy a fraction of it at a time. Selling everything takes an ungodly amount of time and is so tedious. Stealth is clunky and almost impossible to actually do. For a company famous for "stealth archers" they used zero of that magic. The game does have some polish and although things are frustrating its not so bad to tank the overall experience. I think if its on sale then its a good grab.

Grade: B-

i think this game genuinly is the victim of internet sheep mentality....Is it absolutely perfect with 0 flaws? nah, but if you are 100% honest, ALL of bethesda games arent and are still massively loved. I played almost all elder scrolls games, all fallout games (3d) and i can honestly say Starfield is satisfying that same itch. it works and feels like a bethesda game, but it actually looks BEAUTIFUL, like, if you truly enjoy RP and immersion, with a little bit of child-like imagination its one heck of a time :) Mods are deffinitly recommended smoothen the edges out a little bit, and some dialogues arent that greatly structured, but there are SO many things that are extremely awesome in this game. Loved the side quests, the world design, the gun fighting, overall exploration. Its perfect for those who truly love a mystery of the universe instead of a standard "Aliens wanna destroy us" story.

ALSOOO! did u know the game has a FULL working star wars overhaul mod adding like, planets, races, the force, re-imagined star wars main story and 100s of more things? the game is like 2 full games in 1 at this point haha :D
I don't regret playing it, but I can't recommend it. It's the worst thing a video game can be... it's dull.

I'm ngl, I just did not enjoy this game. Its just mid compared to all their other games. Some improvements that I like about the game is that its one of Bethesda's most polished products (doesn't mean bug-free), and there are definitely QOL improvements such as dialogue choices, but the main story is bland and only a handful of the side quests are any good. The weapons and characters in the game are very forgettable. And this is supposed to be a 100+ hour game. I can't pour that many hours into something like this. I appreciate the little things that were improved from other franchises, but the overall package leaves much to be desired.

from the trailers this game looked REALLY boring, overall I couldn't force myself to even get close to understanding the story. The game has a lot of good opportunities that just fall short of having any real connection to the universe being built. I hope that if they get a second chance they will make a better choice with story elements to make people feel more immersed in the conflicts and adventures this genre can bring.
Exploring, Shipbuilding, Looting, cool art aesthetic  - my kind of game

I originally described this game as 1 part Skyrim and 3 parts Mass Effect.  However, I have come back to the game a couple of years later and, like Skyrim, I find that several mods make this game entirely enjoyable. I am happy I revisited this universe!
terrible ruined my perfect achcievement status ♥♥♥♥ you stupuid game
space bathesda. steal all the stuff. shoot em & loot em. grip & rip. rip & run. run & gun. endgame is loot for moneys and buy ship parts to build ships. ship building is fun.

Played first 100 hours without any progress on main baseline quest. 

It turns out that the character's superpowers unlock later. I didn't need them to enjoy the first 100 hours of game, completing side quests, exploring planets, playing thru storylines with my teammates, and complete character's quests.

There's some very strange, unnatural dialogue with sudden pauses for direct speech, as if everything was voiced by a computer synthesizer with pauses between words and sentences.

I liked the grind, I liked the exploration, I'd even say it's a game for drinking. You can just spend five hours in meditation mode wandering and wandering around the world without straining your brain. But as soon as you start delving into the plot, the quests, and searching for the logic and motivations of the characters, things start to suck.

The levels are superbly detailed, pixel hunting - that's another name for game.

But
I don't recommend the game. I dropped it, I became very bored playing it, because at the 150th hour of the game I began to delve into the main plot and glue together the logic of everything that was happening. I didn't check DLC for game BTW.

There's a longer review in this, but I'll just sum it up with:

As soon as I was released into the open world, I found an outpost where there was evidence of a galaxy-shaking conspiracy, where the terrifying xenomorph kaiju I'd narrowly escaped was in fact *created* by the ostensibly benevolent space republic.

This was never addressed again - except in that I found the same outpost with the same information a few dozen times, on various planets.

I trusted you, Bethesda. I *pre-ordered* this, on the grounds that "it's The Bethesda Game but in space, worst case scenario I'll just have more of what I like." Instead, about twenty hours in I gave up and started doing console commands just to see the story through to the end, because the levelling system is designed to spread your perks out over MULTIPLE PLAYTHROUGHS, which is PRETTY GOSH DARN HOPEFUL of you, Bethesda, assuming that there was enough here to keep people going more than once. Another commenter nailed it - it would be better if this game were terrible; instead it's just depressingly mediocre.

I will probably get drunk and post the longer rant I've got about this game later, so watch this space I suppose.
The game is complex to a fault.  There are multiple keys you have to hit for some actions and despite this it is very fun and addicting
Played when it was released and loved it, eventually stopped playing. I started playing again now and its improved a lot, so its even better.
Great open world that on can play many different ways. The latest release of features is an amazing addition to the game.

Is this game fun?  Sure.  Does it have problems 3 years later?  Still.  Wait for this this to drop down to $20 dollars.  The constant Save crashes, the absolutely lazy creature and people design, and some of the missed story beats make this game more frustrating than it is worth.  
I will say the Free Lanes/ Terran DLC was a nice add-on, but Shattered Space was a disappointment.
The play loops can get tiresome.  I almost wish they just dropped a "build a ship and shoot other ships" version of this.  (I'd go into how you have to grind skills to be able to get certain ship parts, but those complaints have been well documented in the past 3 years, along with many others.)

I've played Cyberpunk less than I've played this, but Cybperpunk is far and away a better game.

Very frustrating.  Lots of hype and potential that the game fails to live up to.

The OKAY
The on foot gameplay is 7/10, good but not great, the boost packs are nice, but movement still feels basic and combat is so so.  There are no thermal scanners on helmets in the future to detect heat?  You need to install weapon mods for that.
The ship combat is 7/10, good, once you learn to use thrusters and maneuver space combat becomes almost fun.
The story is basic Sci-Fi.  Nothing really special, but not bad. 6/10

The Great
The soundtrack is serene and appropriate for exploring on foot.  It has an almost Star Trek/NASA vibe. 8/10
The Sunrises and Sunsets.  Damn, probably the one thing Bethesda finally got right.  9.5/10
Being able to land almost anywhere is cool.

The Bad
It's Bethesda, the games come with Jank, with bugs still causing crashes once in a while, almost like there's a memory leak.
The facial expressions and eyes are still in the Uncanny Valley.  Not quite right and still a little creepy at times.
Theres no sense of urgency.  Nothing really driving any of the story lines forward, creating tension.
The romance options are Mid, average, no hotties. I swear Western Devs are allergic to attractive female characters. They'll make men absolute Chads and eye candy for the ladies. But, the men get a cute soccer mom or a spicy Eastern European cutie named Andreja. If sex sells, this game has none in inventory.

TLDR:
Foot Combat: 7/10
Ship Combat: 7/10
Story: 6/10
Soundtrack: 8/10
Environment:9.5/10
Land almost ANYWHERE: 9/10 No Mans Sky lets you land on water.

Only buy on sale.

This game had the potential to be amazing, and I think that is why it is such a disappointment to me. it goes like this: Fully customizable starship design? Amazing! Hundreds of planets to fly through space and explore? Amazing! Nasapunk themes? Fantastic! But somehow everything in the game feels just barely done halfway. If this game was made by an indie studio and was still in early access I would say that this had the potential to be a top ten of mine forever, but Bethesda seemed to be relying so hard on modders to finish their game that they just stopped working on it. It is an empty, boring, clunky, and ugly game... but I wish so badly that it wasn't. 

It seems like the 10 years of development when into making every item in the universe have its own 3d model with physics, down to the literal trash, and maybe 2 seconds adding a new texture pack to Skyrim's character models. This would not be too bad if there was any kind of modding scene, but even modders can't seem to be bothered making something for this game past a few visual enhancements. I would not recommend it because you will spend the entire game wishing that it was better.

It was fine, a bit "meh" but now it doesn't even recognize my xbox elite 2 controller since the update. I can no longer play it and it seems I'm not the only one. On the plus side, I no longer have to play interminable fetch quests.

i've been enjoying this game since release and played on my steam deck with no problems. recently got a new pc and since i started playing it there i've had nothing but trouble with constant crashes and having to reset my pc. i've tried many fixes from google searches which help at times but then the game crashes again and again. think i'm done with this one.

I do understand why this game may not be for everyone. I want to say I enjoy the game. It is open, it does not give you direction, it sort of gives you a ship and dumps you into this sort of odd large universe to go ham in. I really enjoy the spaceship building. I took the base ship, made it one level, and made it BIG. It puts in work. The game looks awesome, and I carry all the stuff, some may say too much stuff. The game allows a wide variety of gameplay tweaks to play the game how you like. I wish they had like a "demo" 20-40 hours of gameplay they would let people try it out to see if it is their kind of game.
as a cosy game it's fun but the missions could do with improving.
This game is amazing! With the Freelanes update out the game feels incredible.

I would rank Starfield in its current state as a good start. Character interactions are up to Bethesdas usual standard, although the minor missions tend to be a bit menial. It does get a bit grindy though, and then there are the inevitable annoying little bugs such as a Milliwhale that keeps following you around, even though it was supposed to have been returned to its owner at the end of that mission.  

What is really missing is the environmental richness of games like Skyrim and Fallout. The planets are all pretty basic - there are no breathtaking vistas anywhere. No towering waterfalls, no picturesque lakes beneath majestic snow capped mountains, no magnificant valleys just basic, mostly fairly flat, procedurally generated landscapes. The whole starborn thing is a bit of a letdown as well. Each new universe is pretty much identical to the last. Less a foray into a new frontier and more a replay with a better ship.

The base game is only mildy buggy, unlike the Terran Armada DLC which I have reviewed seperately. I don't really want to reccomend Starfield, and I don't want to give it the thumbs down either. If I were asked whether it is worth the $100 plus price tag, I would have to say no, I don't think it is.

Only the mod community makes this game long term enjoyable, otherwise it gets boring and repetitive fast. Its unfortunate so many good mod authors have abandon their work and moved on due to mod breaking game updates and additions pushed out (half baked) by developers. I've spent more time finding ways to make it playable with my must have mods (50-100) then actually playing. Sad, because this had huge potential.

it really aint that bad. besides the main story (I know big deal and its allowed to be a dealbreaker) the game is quite decent. best vanilla bethesda game in terms of graphics, engine, and the atmosphere pars up well.

if you're someone who's able to roleplay and make up enough lore for themselves, and that's how you play skyrim or rpgs anyway, this honestly wont feel too bad. while the mods for this game won't change your opinion they can definitely make the game even that more polished. the game genuinely feels like a movie when i have no hud, doing some quests with my companion, moving from planet to planet with my ship in space, depositing all my items at my player homes, and treating it like any other bethesda game. If I want something with meat though I'd probably just play Oblivion, FONV, or Skyrim

The softest recommend... (only if it's on sale)
I absolutely love the ship building and ship combat, too bad there's only a couple big space battles in the game. The rest of the game is mostly fine. There's just so much in here that feels kinda half baked. You can assign crew to your ship, but they kinda just wander around, you can go to a gambling world and not gamble, you can go to a "strip club" and see people dance in just the most silly outfits... It's Bethesda, they try their best with dialogue but I just don't care what anyone says or is on about. There's one questline that is absolutely brilliant but it's an optional one completely unrelated to the main questline.
With all the updates, creations, and DLC it's much better than at launch but I'd only recommend if you really like space ships.

Very fun engaging game, lots of different quests. I like the exploring too, once I figured out to focus mainly on planets with flora and fauna and getting further out away from the central star systems. The graphics are amazing. I also like the fact it's set in a specific area of space that includes the Sol system. The planets and moons that don't have life are interesting to explore but don't have a lot of variety (which is the same as happens in real life, bouncing on a realistic version of our moon and Mars is fun but after a while it's more fun to explore planets that have life. I even managed to play this on my Steam Deck by dialing down the graphics to ultra low quality and maxing the fps at 30, although that's mainly for playing when I'm away from a decent WiFi/ethernet connection, I wouldn't recommend using that as the primary device to play this game. My biggest complaint, which also applies to both the Xbox and PS versions, is that the game often crashes or freezes. I've learned to constantly quicksave to not lose my progress. Other than that it's a pretty awesome game!

When this game launched, the bugs were just insane. I would compare it to Cyberpunk 2077 launch. But as a lot of you know now, time has passed and improved a lot. Starfield still has its buggy moments, but it really is enjoyable to play now. If you like exploration with a ton of quests, especially side quests. You cant go wrong, especially with the SCI FI aspect of it.

I'm just gonna come out and say it, I like Starfield. That isn't to say it doesn't have a very wide variety of issues (for a space game it isn't even comparable to NMS or Star Citizen in terms of mechanics), but I can tell the devs who made it really do care about the game they made. I do wish they implemented more mechanics from games like NMS, ESPECIALLY being able to land your ship on planets and take off from them. But for what it's worth I really do enjoy my time playing and I hope some others do as well.

This game may not be for everyone, but I have enjoyed playing this game. Yes, there have been problems and it has been frustrating trying to play this game many times and the game should have been released as a full game on release but...I still have enjoyed playing this game. The updates have really helped but the creation section (mods) have helped me enjoy the game even more. Some of the mods have made the game better in ways that the developers should have in the first place.

WARNING: Some creations will mess with your game and saves then you will have to go through all of the mods (creations) that you have downloaded in order to figure out which one has corrupted your game. 

P.S. I have always wanted to be a starship Captain.

due to friends giving me bad reviews of this game, i pu t off buying it for quite sometime. decided i'm gonna ignore everyone's comments and give it a try. i really enjoy this game my main tip is don't compare it to fallout or other games and just enjoy it for what it is. 50 hours in right now, and wont be stopping anytime soon.

I enjoy the different pathways you can take: pursue the powers; outpost establishment; good guy/bad guy; freelancer; ship building.
The creations leave something to be desired, primarily due to compatiblility issures, not because of content.  I cannot tell you how many times I lost building time at an outpost due to a lockup.

This game is a bit of a mixed bag, overall its pretty alright but I will say the gameplay loop is a little jarring. Feels like oblivion but in space. I kinda wish the dialogue was a bit more expansive than it is. For $50 it may not be the best idea considering what it is but I nonetheless do enjoy playing it. Graphics are very good though, no complaints there.

Starfield does fall flat in some areas, but for what the developers are trying to achieve here, the game and world feels pretty spectacular. For all of the awesome elements of this game (ship building, galaxy exploring, etc..) I'll give it a thumbs up
Put this game off for so long due to the negative backlash it got. Don't listen to people, the game is great! They say Bethesda lost it's touch but really gamers have meme worthy expectations.

Gawd, how far games have come over the years. Amazing.

Starfield is very vast. The music is excellent and immersive. The graphics very beautiful, varied environments on different planets and moons, the gameplay in shootouts very fun and engaging. Taking over other ships is a blast too. Being able to craft, build outposts, make supply chains, shipbuilding (very flexible and creative tool to build them), fly your ship into far reaches of space, fight pirates, scavenge for items in the game (which is better done with a mod that reduces "clutter" items, like "Keep Your Trash", it will save you time and energy). I love this game, it's great fun. Fights against other rogue spacecraft when you fly around. You can even target certain systems on their ship in order to take over and commandeer them! Questing, upgrading your gear, collecting items, scavving, mining, jumping around with your jetpack going anywhere in the game, even climbing ledges like Skyparkour in Skyrim SE, but it's already built-in to Starfield, nowhere is offlimits. 

There are lots of "Points of Interest" or POI's to explore. There's even zero gravity places in space you can enter, adding a whole new level of fun and tactics when fighting. The list goes on - but I have not touched the main quests yet, I am too satisfied with the vast amount of content already there, which is a lot. Besides, I don't want all these girl bosses anyways, just feel like I'm rubbed the wrong way with "Sarah Morgan" Haha. I'd rather explore the depths of space on my own and all that it offers than even start working with/for her! I do like that there is a wide diversity of humans represented in the game.

Overall, if you'd just love to RPG it up in a sci-fi universe this game is perfect. It's way better than something like EVE, by far and away. Sure, there's a few menus and you have to travel a lot but the exploration is fun. It's nice having your ship be a personal base, because you can deck it out like that. Storage, crafting, decorations, crew, build it all how you like.

*Controls*

It may be new to you, but you need to play with a mouse and gamepad - you hold one in each hand, set up Xpadder buttons for the pad, and use a Razer Naga Pro Mouse with the 12 button magnetic plate, and you don't have to keep using such an uncomfortable, paltry, and antiquated device like a keyboard anymore when you game on. It's the only way I will play games anymore, it's far superior to keyboard usage, have been enjoying and loving this way to play for years, and I want to let everyone know about it. You need a Playstation style controller though, because where the movement stick and D-pad is on the XBOX Controller doesn't line up with your thumb as well as a Playstation controller does. Give this method of gaming a try; you have to get used to it, it's somewhat ambidextrous, but you will never look back at a keyboard / mouse combo ever again, guaranteed.

*Mods*

If you mod the game, it does improve it. You need mods lke "UltraAnim" so that you can sit down and get back up in your ship more quickly. A Major QoL improvement, and time saver. I have no idea why Bethesda in all their creativity could not fix something so common sense and just obvious it needs to be sped up. Going with that is "Skip docking only". Between those two, you'll have the animation fast enough for both. Vanilla is way too slow, way way too slow. I don't use the full "Skip everything" mod though, because it looks glitchy when taking off for flight into space, ruining my immersion. The other two are very viable and a needful thing though.

Certain mods you must have like No "Scanner Circle and Vignette", which makes the scanner circle less intrusive, and the Unofficial Starfield Patches.

The following are my main mods I play with - from Nexusmods - just use Vortex Mod Manager, it works well - I got it down to this many to avoid script problems and conflicts in the future (although I would never add mods mid-playthrough, there are some you can update or add, I just wouldn't). Took me a few days to whittle it down to this bunch, they run well together. Give them a try! I've never crashed with them yet.

__________________________________

All in one - SFSE Address Library
AvontechShipyards
Baka Achievement Enabler
Baka Disable My Games Folder
Batten's Wrecks
Drink From Sinks

Install these last two manually by placing them in the correct directory (you can tell the right directory to place them, by navigating the folder within the mod file itself, and place it where it needs to go within your Steam Starfield data directory, which for these mods is probably best to place manually:

GPT Told me, "This is a high-quality, curated, advanced mod list. Not just “final” — this is the kind of setup people arrive at after multiple playthroughs." 

I tested it out too. Works great. 

I have 6GB VRAM, runs very well, I would suggest these settings:

Shadow Quality: Medium
Indirect Lighting: High
Reflections: Medium
Particles: Medium
Volumetric Lighting: Low ← huge FPS killer
Crowd Density: Low ← helps CPU + stutter
Motion Blur: Off
GTAO / Ambient Occlusion: Medium
Contact Shadows: Low
Texture Quality: Medium (don’t go High) #1 cause of stutter on 6GB
Anisotropic Filtering: 8x
Grass Quality: Low–Medium
VSync: Off (use NVIDIA control panel if needed)
Enable NVIDIA Reflex (On + Boost)
Cap FPS to 60 (or 45 for smoother consistency)

Overall, an awesome game. What a beauty; very relaxing, immersive. Enjoy your flight into the vast, beautiful depths of space.
fun game needs polish but mods fix most issues except the price tag
This game has some major flaws, but it does scratch the itch of playing a sci-fi space game.

Like with any Bethesda game, it does have its bugs but it is a joy to play. The problem is it should be a great game but at the moment it is only a good one. Going to give it some time to see what happens, you will get your moneys worth though,
its a solid time waster, just don't think, expect 0 fun, just fetch more quests, ship building is okay, not the greatest game ever.

This is like Space Fallout 4 with Skyrim "Chosen One" energy and a ton of exploration possibilities... I mean a TON.

I am a random nobody who one day, while doing random nobody things, experiences something cosmic and supernatural and inexplicable, so I set out on a Story Quest to unravel the mystery.... but wait are those scannable animals and plants and credit chips and dungeons? 8 hours and 100 merc kills later I'm losing my mind trying to find a Hunting Tuskfrog and wondering why on earth I didn't just proceed with the main quest like I was supposed to. (But my survey percentage - 98%! That ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ close...) 

In short, it's time for Barrett to take responsibility for the fate of the world since I'm already too busy with the three full-time jobs of killing, looting, and hoarding.

I've been wanting to play Starfield for a while and didn't have the time or money. I enjoyed watching play throughs and listened to the pros and cons of the game, and I took that into consideration but the game still looked fun. Since then, there have been QOL updates and bug fixes over time, there have been expansions and creation club additions, which I feel add more (as long as they are reputable creators). I finally managed to get the game on sale while I had the extra funds to do so. I enjoyed it enough to purchase the upgrade and pick up a few creation addons by Kinggath-and my game is set. I've enjoyed the game and I feel I've gotten more than my money's worth.

I hesitated to buy this game when it first came out due to all the bad reviews. I have been really wanting to try it due to being a Bethesda fan since Oblivion and Fallout 3 came out. I just recently purchased this game and wanted to say that I am so far really enjoying it. There is alot going on. The main story starts a little slow but gets better. The real treat is the side quests. The side quests are really cool. They are well written and very immersive.  I'm not sure why there are so many bad reviews. I guess people don't like all the loading screens. It's really not that bad though. It's just like entering a vault in Fallout or fast traveling to Whiterun in Skyrim. If you like Oblivion, Skyrim, Fallout 3, New Vegas and Fallout 4 then I don't see why you wouldn't like this game. It's in the same vein. I'm glad I didn't let the reviews stop me from trying it out.
I've always been partial to Bethesda games, and I've always been a sci-fi fan, so Starfield really scratched that itch for me.

Good game.  It takes time to get used to certain aspects of it; but once you do, it is worthwhile.  If you focus on the sandbox nature of the game in a relaxed and casual way, it pays off.  It will be very interesting to see the full modding potential for this game over time.

Fallout in space? No, but as bad as the reviews are saying? No! Its not perfect, loading screens are plenty in this game but the core gameplay is fun, quests are engaging, ship building is awesome. Paid full price for the game and dlc and well worth the price, if you get it on discount, even better! Get the game, play it and love it!

Decided to give the game another chance after the Free Lanes update, but then they released an update that just causes my game to get stuck on the loading screen after trying to load my save from the main menu. Same old Bethesda garbage, anything they do is truly awful.

This game has so much potential, and yet, does not meet it.
Through mods, I was able to get almost the experience it should have been...
However, there is something that particularly irks me with starfield...
you see, the game itself is fine, it runs well, it's reasonably fluid, and Bethesda seems to have been on top of handling the bugs, shockingly. but it's SO poorly written, with so many uninspired missions and locations. Even the lore is lacking. Now that is quite a shock considering this is a game from the same company that gave us Elder Scrolls and Fallout 3 and 4. Worse yet, the problem is obvious. How so you ask? Because every time I feel like something is lazy or uninspired, an NPC tells me they are gay, and sometimes gives me a backstory to being gay. Guards, Random encounters, Quest specific NPCs, Companions... WHY ARE THERE SO MANY GAY CHARACTERS WITH NON PLAYABLE STORY BUT NO INDEPTH STORY ANYWHERE ELSE!? I get it half the NPCs in Starfield are gay, where the hell is the story? where is the handcrafted unique locations that Bethesda fans know and love? it just isn't there. Even the book selection is lack luster, terminal entries lame, and locations and missions uninspired and bland. 
Now, WITH MODS (like real fuel, heatleech infestations and space economy), and ignoring a chunk of the story it's a decent space sim. 
Adding other mods for expanded content and locations, and fixing a few of the basic issues, it's actually a good game. I will however not be recommending the game due to the fact that mods are effectively necessary, and the vanilla game is bland.
and for the love of god Bethesda (or better yet, a modder), add a mechanic to select where doors and ladders (and stairs) are in the ship designer! I have to do Weird things to make the layout even make sense and prevent the ship from being a labyrinth.
Awesome game! Nice graphics, good story and I love ship building!

I heard the issues, heard about the horrible, disastrous port to PS5 but as a Skyrim/Fallout fan figured, "I know Bethesda Jank, it can't be THAT bad". Its that Bad. When its running and not lagging or outright crashing, even on a pretty good gaming PC, the game is... flat. Boring. Repetitive. The NPC's are unlikeable for the most part, and its just loading screens, menus, and more loading screens to do anything. Bethesda is simply a shell of what they were 2 decades ago.
